content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
June 11,
1993
Marie Mime
Office of Planning
and Research
1400 10th Street,
Room 150
Sacramento,
CA 95814
RE:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No.
A-93—214
Dear Ms. Milne:
This is in response to your request for advice regarding
whether the California Military Base Reuse Task Force
(CMBRTF)
is
an “agency” subject to the provisions of the Political Reform Act
(“the Act”.)
1
’
QUESTIONS
1.
Must
CMBRTF adopt a conflict of interest code?
2.
Must members of
CMBRTF and it staff file statements of
economic interests?
CONCLUSIONS
1.
CMBRTF has no obligation to adopt a conflict of interest
code.
2.
The members of CNBRTF are not required to file
statements of economic interests.
Members of the
CMBRTF staff
have no obligation to file statements of economic interests by
virtue of their positions with CMBRTF.
1/
Government Code Sections 81000—91015.
All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000,
q.
All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95804-0807 •
(916) 322-5860


Mime, Marie
No.
A—93—214
Page
2
FACTS
CMBRTF was created on April 14,
1993 by Executive Order W-50-
93 for the purpose of promoting the speedy conversion of closing
California military bases and maximizing their contribution to the
State’s economy.
CMBRTF was established to:
a)
make recoimuendations regarding proposed state and
federal legislation and executive actions which would
eliminate government imposed obstacles to converting
military bases to civilian uses or streamline base reuse
procedures.
b)
develop a broad strategy for converting all closing
bases that maximizes their total value for creating jobs
and economic activity.
c)
assist state and local efforts in marketing base reuse
plans to the federal government and to private
developers.
CMBRTF will consist of a chairperson and the following
members,
each selected by the Governor:
1)
two representatives of local governments;
2)
one representative of banking or financial institutions;
3)
two representatives of industrial development interests;
4)
one public member with expertise in toxic clean—up
issues;
5)
one representative from a labor union.
All state departments are directed to cooperate with CMBRTF
and provide assistance as necessary.
The Office of Planning and Research, the California
Environmental Protection Agency, the Trade and Commerce Agency,
and the Business, Transportation,
and Housing Agency will provide
staff assistance to CMBRTF.
CMBRTF will hold hearings throughout the State and make its
final recommendations to the Governor by August 31,
1993.
Interim
recommendations may be made as appropriate.
It is my understanding pursuant to our telephone
conversations on May 27,
1993 that not all members of CMBRTF have
been named,
no hearings have been held,
and no recommendations
have been made.


Mime, Marie
No.
A—93—214
Page
3
ANALYSIS
Government Code Section 87300 requires every agency to adopt
and promulgate a conflict of interest code.
A conflict of
interest code is a document which specifically enumerates each
position within the agency which involves the making or
participation in the making of decisions which may foreseeably
have a material effect on a financial interest and for each such
enumerated position, requires disclosure of specific types of
investments, business positions, interests in real property,
and
sources of income.
An investment, business position, interest in
real property, or source of income must be made reportable by the
conflict of interest code if the business entity in which the
investment or business position is held, the interest in real
property,
or the income or source of income may foreseeably be
affected materially by an decision made or participated in by the
designated employee by virtue of his or her position.
(Section
87302.)
The term “designated employee” means any officer,
employee,
member or consultant of any agency whose position with the agency
is designated in a conflict of interest code because the position
entails the making or participation in the making of decisions
which may foreseeably have a material effect on any financial
interest.
The term “designated employee” does not include any
unsalaried member of any board or commission which serves a solely
advisory function.
(Section 82019.)
“Member” includes any salaried or unsalaried member of a
board or commission with decision—making authority.
A board or
commission possesses decision—making authority whenever:
(A)
It may make a final government decision;
(B)
It may compel a governmental decision; or may prevent a
governmental decision either by reason of an exclusive
power to initiate the decision or by reason of a veto
which may not be overridden; or
(C)
It makes substantive recommendations which are,
and over
an extended period of time have been, regularly approved
without significant amendment or modification by another
public official or governmental agency.
Regulation 18700(a) (1).
It does not appear that CMBRTF will be making any final
governmental decisions.
CMBRTF will make recommendations to the
Governor, but it. does not appear that he is bound by those
recommendations.
Therefore, it appears that CMBRTF serves a


Mime, Marie
No.
A—93—214
Page
4
solely advisory function.
2
’
1
If you have additional questions, please contact me at
(916)
322—5662.
Sincerely,
Jeff Marschner
General Cou
el
w
By:
ayne P.
Imberi
Political Reform Consultant
2 /
Under Regulation 18700 (a) (1) (C), members of CMBRTF may become
public officials subject to the Act’s requirements if their
recommendations over an extended period of time are routinely
approved without significant amendment or modification by another
public official or government agency.
However, since it is our
understanding that CNBRTF will expire on August 31,
1993 we assume
that this portion of the regulation will not apply.
If
CMBRTF
continues to function for a period significantly after that date
you should contact us for additional advice.

2.
Must members of

economic interests?

code.
2.

CMBRTF staff
have no obligation to file statements of economic interests by
virtue of their positions with CMBRTF.
1,
q.
All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
 •
(916) 322-5860


Mime, Marie
No.
A—93—214
